name: Why bees pollinate so much of what we eat
description:
### The headline
More than 75% of the world's leading food crops depend, at least in part, on animal pollination, and most of that work is done by honeybees and their wild cousins.

### Why it matters
Without pollinators, the global supply of fruit, vegetables, nuts, and oilseeds would shrink dramatically. The yield of a single almond orchard depends on hives shipped in from hundreds of miles away during bloom. Coffee, cocoa, apples, blueberries, melons — all owe their abundance to bees moving pollen from flower to flower as a side-effect of foraging for nectar.

### What is at stake
Bee colonies have collapsed in waves over the past two decades, from pesticide exposure, habitat loss, and disease. Researchers track the link between bee health and crop yields year on year. A few percentage points of pollinator decline can translate to millions of dollars of lost produce.

### What helps
Native-flower verges along roads, reduced neonicotinoid use, beekeeper collaboration with farmers, and home gardens that include wild-flower patches. Small actions, scaled, matter.
